name: Órganos de Montoro Loop from Castellote
name_original: Organos de Montoro - circular desde Castellote
description: This difficult 69.4-mile road cycling route from Castellote features the dramatic Órganos de Montoro and scenic sections of "The Silent Rout
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Castellote are characterized by the varied terrain of the Maestrazgo region in Teruel, Spain. The area features a mix of hills and valleys, providing diverse topography for cyclists. Notable natural elements include the fluvio-karstic formations of Puente de Fonseca and the dramatic gorges of the Guadalope River. The municipality's mountainous character offers numerous climbs, making it suitable for demanding road cycling experiences. Best road cycling routes around Castellote The most popular road cycling route is Castellote and Mas de las Mat, a 32.7 miles (52.6 km) trail that takes 2 hours 44 minutes to complete. This difficult route connects two key locations, traversing varied landscapes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Órganos de Montoro Loop from Castellote, a difficult 69.4 miles (111.6 km) path. This route explores the rugged landscapes and dramatic geological formations of the Órganos de Montoro. Local road cyclists also love the Castellote Village and Castle – Forcall loop from Mas de las Matas, a 55.5 miles (89.3 km) trail leading through historic Castellote village and its castle, often completed in about 4 hours 29 minutes. Road cycling around Castellote is defined by the Maestrazgo region's hills and valleys, the gorges of the Guadalope River, and mountainous terrain. The network offers options for both moderate and difficult ability levels. The routes in Castellote are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from more than 6 reviews. More than 500 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Castellote's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Castellote?
A: There are over 40 road cycling routes available around Castellote, offering a diverse range of experiences. Most routes are categorized as moderate to difficult, catering to cyclists looking for a challenge in the varied terrain of the Maestrazgo region.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Castellote?
A: Road cycling in Castellote features a mix of hills and valleys characteristic of the Maestrazgo region. You'll encounter numerous climbs due to the area's mountainous character, along with dramatic natural features like the gorges of the Guadalope River and the unique fluvio-karstic formations of Puente de Fonseca.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: Yes, Castellote is well-suited for experienced road cyclists, with 31 routes rated as difficult. A notable challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e959216401/organos-de-montoro-loop-from-castellote" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Órganos de Montoro Loop from Castellote&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 111.6 km with significant elevation gain, exploring rugged landscapes and dramatic geological formations.

Q: What natural attractions can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer stunning natural sights. You can cycle past the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5355841" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Estrecho de Crespol Gorge&lt;/a&gt;, or experience the unique geological formations of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2088068" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Órganos de Montoro Pass&lt;/a&gt;. The region is also known for the Puente de Fonseca Natural Monument, a natural tunnel carved by the Guadalope River.

Q: Are there historical landmarks or charming villages to visit on a road cycling tour?
A: Absolutely. Castellote itself is a declared Historic Site, dominated by the remains of a Templar castle. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20144096" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castellote Village and Castle – Forcall loop from Mas de las Matas&lt;/a&gt; will take you through the historic village. You might also pass through charming smaller villages such as Cuevas de Cañart, Abenfigo, and Luco de Bordón, each offering a glimpse into traditional architecture.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Castellote?
A: Given Castellote's mountainous character and varied terrain, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling. These seasons provide milder temperatures, ideal for tackling the region's ascents and descents while enjoying the natural beauty without the extreme heat of summer or the cold of winter.

Q: Are there any moderate road cycling routes suitable for less experienced riders?
A: Yes, while many routes are difficult, there are 11 moderate options available. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20143848" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mas de las Matas – Castellote loop from Mas de las Matas&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 34.4 km route, offering a good balance of challenge and scenic beauty without the intensity of the more difficult trails.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes in Castellote?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Castellote are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e959216401/organos-de-montoro-loop-from-castellote" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Órganos de Montoro Loop from Castellote&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20143858" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Calanda Reservoir – Mas de las Matas loop from Mas de las Matas&lt;/a&gt;, providing convenient options for exploration.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in Castellote?
A: The road cycling routes in Castellote are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from more than 6 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the dramatic natural landscapes, and the immersive experience of riding through this peaceful region.

Q: Where can I find parking when starting a road cycling route in Castellote?
A: Castellote village itself offers parking facilities, making it a convenient starting point for many road cycling adventures. From the village, you can easily access routes that explore the surrounding Maestrazgo region and its natural and historical attractions.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of reservoirs or other water bodies?
A: Yes, some routes provide scenic views of local water bodies. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20143858" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Calanda Reservoir – Mas de las Matas loop from Mas de las Matas&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate route that offers picturesque vistas of the Calanda Reservoir, combining cycling with beautiful waterside scenery.
tips: The first time we see the town of Villarluengo, it will leave us speechless due to its location, as it is located on top of a rocky hill next to a deep ravine. If we go through Villarluengo it is worth visiting the Balcony of the Forasteros, the Town Hall building, the parish church or the Hermitages of San Bartolomé and San Cristóbal. If we feel like enjoying nature, we can visit the natural monument of Los Órganos de Montoro or the port of Cuarto Pelado.
Road uphill that goes in the direction of the town of Villarluengo from the Guadalope river valley, passing through the Organos de Montoro
Between the Teruel towns of Ejulve and Villarluengo, the Órganos de Montoro appear, geological formations that receive this name because their shape is similar to the "pipes" of an organ.
Hard pass but without difficulty, the last of the Silence route.

The climb from Cueva de Cañart to Torre de las Monjas is an ascent where you leave the town at your back but with great views of the valley. You reach the plain of Val de l'Agustina, which is an elevated plain above the Sierra de la Garrucha.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as difficult and requires very good fitness. It features significant length (approximately 112 km) and substantial elevation gain (around 2,480 meters), making it challenging for inexperienced riders.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this circular road cycling route?
faqs.answer: Given its distance and elevation, the route typically takes around 6.5 hours of active cycling. However, this can vary based on your fitness level, speed, and how many stops you make to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: What are the main natural features and scenic highlights along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers breathtaking views through the interior of Teruel. A major highlight is the Órganos de Montoro, a Natural Monument with dramatic vertical escarpments. You'll also traverse sections of "The Silent Route" (A-1702 between Villarluengo and Ejulve), known for its impressive canyons and picturesque views. Other notable points include Puerto de Villarluengo and Puerto de Los Degollaos.

faqs.question: Where is the best place to park and start this circular route from Castellote?
faqs.answer: The route is a circular one starting from Castellote. You can typically find parking within the town of Castellote itself, which serves as a convenient starting and ending point for the loop.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Maestrazgo region can experience varied weather. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the colder conditions of winter. Always check the local weather forecast before you go.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route, primarily on paved roads. While there are no specific restrictions against dogs on public roads, it's generally not advisable to bring dogs on long, challenging road cycling tours due to safety concerns for both the dog and the cyclist. If you plan to stop in towns, be mindful of local leash laws and pet policies.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this route or the Maestrazgo Geopark?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need special permits or to pay entrance fees to cycle this route or to access the Maestrazgo Geopark. The natural formations and roads are publicly accessible. However, always respect local regulations and private property.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is a hard road ride with generally well-paved surfaces. However, it is characterized by mountainous terrain with significant and demanding climbs, often reaching over 1000 meters in elevation. Expect a constant challenge with ascents and descents.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly overlaps with sections of other official trails. You will encounter parts of the Sierra de los Caballos - Santolea, [CIMA TE09] Cuarto Pelado, El Raspador, and Hoces del Guadalope trails, among others, for short segments.

faqs.question: Are there any places to stop for food or coffee along the way?
faqs.answer: While the route passes through some small settlements like Villarluengo, services can be sparse in this mountainous region. It's highly recommended to carry sufficient water and snacks, and plan your stops carefully. Castellote, your starting point, will have more options.

faqs.question: What should I pack for this challenging road cycling tour?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and length, you should pack essential cycling gear, including a helmet, appropriate cycling attire, repair kit, spare tubes, and a pump. Carry plenty of water, energy gels or bars, and a first-aid kit. A fully charged phone and a GPS device are also crucial for navigation and safety.

waypoints.name: Ascent to the Torre de las Monjas
